APPROPRIATENESS.justification: This paper addresses within-document event linking (event coreference) using convolutional neural networks and standard datasets (ACE 2005 and ACE++) and explicitly reports state-of-the-art performance, which falls squarely within the core interests of top NLP venues (see Sections 4–5 and the conclusion). The methods (CNN-based sentential feature extraction, trainable similarity, use of pre-trained embeddings, Adam optimization) and evaluation setup (ACE, comparison to prior work, error analysis) follow the empirical standards expected at top NLP/ML conferences. The related work discussion situates the contribution among existing event-coreference and neural relation-classification research, indicating correct positioning for the target community.

SOUNDNESS_CORRECTNESS.justification: The proposed model is technically plausible and the mathematical formulations shown (Eq.4–6, logistic loss, Adam optimization) are correct, but the paper as provided lacks crucial experimental detail to fully support its main claims. The authors state they achieve state-of-the-art performance on two datasets (Section 5) yet no quantitative results, baseline comparisons, or statistical significance testing are shown in the excerpt. There are also unsupported methodological choices (creation and annotation quality of ACE++ is not described; clustering uses a hard 0.5 threshold with transitive closure—Figure 3—without justification; the squared activation is motivated by intuition only). Overall the approach seems sound but the evidence in the provided text is insufficient to fully validate the claimed empirical improvements.

IMPACT.justification: The paper addresses an important problem—within-document event linking/coreference—which "enables extraction systems to process document-level information" and is central to producing coherent event instances (intro/summary). By proposing a neural method that "does not rely on [external knowledge] but instead utilizes sentential features coming from convolutional neural networks," and by showing it "achieves state-of-the-art performance on two datasets, one of which is publicly available," the work is likely to influence how researchers build event-coreference components in IE pipelines. The creation/use of an expanded ACE++ corpus and the model’s avoidance of heavy external KB features open practical and research directions, though the impact is focused within the event/coreference subfield rather than broadly transformative.

MEANINGFUL_COMPARISON.justification: The paper explicitly compares to state-of-the-art systems on ACE (Section 5) and discusses many relevant prior event-coreference approaches (Section 6, e.g., Chen and Ji 2009; Lee et al. 2012; Cybulska and Vossen 2015; Araki and Mitamura 2015). It also performs ablation/variant comparisons on their extended ACE++ dataset and presents an error analysis. However, the provided text lacks explicit detail that all baselines were run under identical conditions or which evaluation metrics were used, and it does not clearly acknowledge cases where prior methods may match or outperform their model.

ORIGINALITY.justification: The paper applies convolutional neural networks to generate sentential features for within-document event coreference, explicitly avoiding external KB features ("does not rely on such features but instead utilizes sentential features coming from convolutional neural networks"). This is a clear differentiation from much prior work that used hand-crafted compatibility metrics or rich semantic resources (e.g., Chen and Ji 2009; Lee et al. 2012), but the core technical novelty is moderate — it adapts CNN sentence representations and a trainable similarity layer (including a squared nonlinearity) to a well-studied task rather than introducing a fundamentally new framework. Overall the contribution is a meaningful, incremental advance that would be informative to experts but not radically novel.

CLARITY.justification: The paper is generally well organized and follows a logical flow (Problem definition → model details in 3.2 → training and experiments → related work), with concrete examples (the Barclays example) helping to motivate the task. The main contribution is described clearly, but only near the end (final paragraph beginning “Coreference resolution for event mentions…/We propose a model…”), so it is not stated early as would be preferable. Notation around the model equations (Eqns. 4–6) is mostly consistent and defined, though some LaTeX/artifact formatting (e.g., “intermin-gles”, “wrt.”, stray TeX tokens) and missing inline figures/tables references (Figure 1(b), Figure 3, Table 1/2 are cited but not shown here) slightly hinder readability. Overall the writing is clear with only minor presentation and formatting issues.

SUBSTANCE.justification: The paper evaluates on ACE and an expanded ACE++ dataset and reports comparisons to state-of-the-art systems, with a non-trivial hyperparameter exploration (random sampling of 180 points and line search) and final settings (Table 2) selected on validation. The authors state they contrast model variations to study the impact of feature classes (i.e., ablation-style comparisons) and include an error analysis discussing directions for future work. Related work and dataset resources are covered broadly and fairly (multiple prior methods and corpora are cited). Reproducibility is fairly well supported (TensorFlow implementation, ACE splits, training regime, parameter set θ, use of pre-trained embeddings), though some low-level hyperparameter values are only referenced to Table 2 (minor remaining detail gap).

REPLICABILITY.justification: Many crucial reproducibility details are missing from the provided text: the final hyperparameter values and model configuration are only said to be in Table 2 (not present), so sizes (dw, dp, dc, de, dsim), convolution window(s), minibatch size, learning-rate and Adam (η, β1, β2) values, regularization choices, and other training choices are unspecified. The data preprocessing pipeline and whether gold event mentions/arguments are used is not described, and the ACE++ dataset (authors’ augmentation of ACE) is not provided or documented here. No code release is given, and evaluation metrics/procedures are not precisely defined in the excerpt. These omissions make faithful reproduction difficult.
